document.critique: Tested all constraints and machine counts. Candidate 0 survives: each machine has three jobs, all pairwise/conditional restrictions hold, and both exact-one conditions are satisfied. Its cost sums to 130. Candidate 4 is infeasible because J10 cannot use Z; the other feasible candidates cost more (142, 146, and 148). Thus candidate 0 is retained as the unique optimum.

I rechecked capacity, all pairwise/conditional rules, both exact-one constraints, and recalculated every candidate’s cost. Candidate 3 is feasible and totals 124, not its stated 135; the others are feasible but cost 135, 138, 154, and 136 respectively. Exhaustive structural checking of the J6=J9 cases found no lower feasible assignment, so Candidate 3’s corrected result replaces the preceding proposal.

Tested machine counts, all pair/conditional constraints, both exact-one rules, and recomputed costs. Candidate 1 is feasible with three jobs per machine and cost 143. Candidate 0 is feasible but costs 144; Candidate 2 violates capacities; Candidate 3 violates the J7=Y implication because J5=Z. Thus Candidate 1 should replace the preceding proposal.

Recomputed all listed totals and checked capacities and every conditional/exact-one rule. Candidates 0–4 either misstate their arithmetic or are costlier; Candidate 0’s assignment actually costs 148, and Candidate 1 costs 141. The returned assignment is feasible and totals 115, so it replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated each candidate by requiring every statement's Boolean value to equal its proposed type. Candidate 0 survives most direct checks, including A–L and N–Q, but M's OR-expression evaluates true and P's AND-expression evaluates false, so it is not actually self-consistent. The alternatives likewise fail direct checks (typically at least two). Thus no candidate is fully validated; returning Candidate 0 is only a best-fit retention, not a proof of the requested unique solution.

Rechecked every candidate against the fixed-point rule that each statement’s Boolean value equals its speaker’s type; all five candidates fail (notably Candidate 0 fails at I). Solving the constraints yields the returned assignment. Direct substitution makes each truthful statement true and each liar statement false, so this exact answer replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated every nested Boolean statement under Candidate 0, including EXACTLY counts, XORs, and liar falsity. All 17 statement truth values match their speakers’ assigned types. The other candidates fail at least B or H; thus Candidate 0 survives and is the exact answer.

I re-evaluated each candidate by substituting its T-values into every nested statement. Candidates 0–3 each fail at least one direct truth/statement equivalence; candidate 4 survives all checked equations except the M clause, which evaluates true under the listed values. Thus the supplied choices appear inconsistent, but candidate 4 is the strongest surviving proposal and is retained provisionally.

I rechecked the period-4 recurrence against the supplied terms and compared the three possible moduli, testing the restricted coefficient values and affine term constraints. The zero and repeated-term candidates fail immediately as continuations; [65, 41, 47] is the surviving proposed continuation and should replace the absent preceding proposal.

Re-derived the recurrence rather than trusting either candidate. The surviving system has M=101, t=2, r=-1, s=3, with relevant coefficients p1=1,q1=-1; p2=2,q2=-1; p3=-1,q3=-2; p0=2,q0=-2. It reproduces the sequence through a17 and yields a18=22, a19=82, a20=50. Both candidate outputs fail verification.

Re-derived the system modulo 103: t=-2, r=1, s=4, with residue-class coefficients including (p0,q0)=(-2,-1), (p2,q2)=(-2,-1), and (p3,q3)=(2,2). These reproduce all given terms; applying the recurrence at n=18,19,20 yields 92, 2, 18. Thus Candidate 3 survives and replaces the others.
